story:
Jamyria

“You know what? I quit, Mr. Muse. I ain’t doing this.” That’s what I said, staring him down, unsure if I should smile or not. Everyone else had already paired up and started. Da’Vonne and Bailey were giggling and working, the Aidens had teamed up, even Gavyn and Kelsey were trying, and I knew they didn’t know nothing. My real problem? When Mr. Muse told us to find partners, I sat still like I didn’t hear. Whoops.

Mr. Muse

I don’t do coffee, alcohol, or anything like that, so my highs come from other places. That day’s high was that Trey was absent, which usually made class run smoother. But seeing every kid on task except Jamyria made me want to grab a chair and park it beside her. If her partner couldn’t be here, I’d be the partner.

Jamyria

Mr. Muse squeezed into the desk next to me. “Looks like there’s not enough partners for you,” he said.

“Oh, yeah,” I said, fake casual. “Guess I can’t do the problem now, right?”

“You know it don’t work like that.” He tapped the worksheet. “Let’s read and see what we know.”

He waited. “You know when I say, ‘Let’s read,’ I mean you read.”

I read: “Will read 15 more pages than Marcy. Marcy read 38 pages. The book is 82 pages long.”

“This is a two-step word problem,” he said. “What do they want us to find first?”

“How many pages did Will read,” I said.

“Okay. So how many pages did Will read?”

“Fifteen.”

“You sure?”

“It says it right there,” I said. He gave me that look. I read again and went, “Ohhhh.”

“Read it out loud.”

“Will read 15 more pages than Marcy.”

“So how many pages did Will read?”

“Fifteen more.”

“Is that an exact number?”

“No, sir.”

“Okay then. Will read 15 more than who?”

“Marcy. She read 38 pages,” I said, feeling good to jump ahead.

“What model have we been using to show adding more to numbers?”

“Why I gotta be psychic, Mr. Muse?”

“It is what it is. What do we use?”

“A tape diagram!”

“Right. Draw it.”

I drew Marcy’s 38, then added a 15-length piece for Will. “I’m adding. Will read 53 pages. Tell me I’m wrong.”

“I can’t. Now the last part: how many more pages does Will need to finish the book?”

“You’re dirty, Mr. Muse. But okay.” I sighed. “Why they got so much reading in a math problem? It’s almost summer.”

“Focus. What do you know about the book?”

“It’s 82 pages long! Nobody’s reading that.” He gave me that look again.

“What has to happen?”

“Will has to get from 53 to 82. I gotta figure out how many more.”

“We’re almost home.”

“It’s a half-day?” I asked.

“That’s a saying. Two ways?”

“I can count on or subtract. Counting on’s easier.”

“Count, baby, count.”

I counted up from 53 to 82. “Twenty-nine more. Do Will need 29 more?”

“Hallelujah, we are done,” he said.

I started the Dora the Explorer beat: “We did it, we did it, we did it, yeah! Lo hicimos, we did it!” I added, “We solved this math problem, yeah we got it straight!”

The class jumped in with their own remix, clapping and laughing, a couple paper airplanes gliding by and kids cracking jokes. Mr. Muse watched, smiling that big teacher smile that means playtime’s over. “All of y’all, sit y’all behinds down!”

We dropped into our seats, heads down to be cute. And I couldn’t help it—I love my class.
